layer1.what: Opens the 6-1-6 Mapping panel where you tokenize text through the anchor pipeline.
layer1.why: Use this when you want to map a document's words to Forest AI's canonical lexicon using the 6-words-before, 1-anchor, 6-words-after window.
layer1.consequence: Switches the visible workspace to the mapping panel. Any in-progress work in other panels stays in memory.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Lexicon Browser panel where you can search, browse, and manage your word database.
layer1.why: Use this to inspect canonical words, see letter distribution, review recently mapped entries, or manage partitions.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the lexicon panel and loads current stats. Random entries are shown on first load.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the main chat panel where you interact with the connected language model.
layer1.why: This is your primary workspace for sending prompts, viewing responses, and managing conversations.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the chat panel. Your conversation history from today is visible and persists across panel switches.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Inference Control panel where you tune model generation parameters like temperature, top-p, and repeat penalty.
layer1.why: Use this to adjust how the AI generates responses. Different tasks benefit from different parameter profiles.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the inference panel and loads the current parameter values for the active model.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the System & Identity panel where you define the AI's persona and your user profile.
layer1.why: The system identity shapes every response. Your user profile provides context so the AI tailors its output to your expertise and workflow.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the identity panel and loads current system prompt, archive instruction, and user profile fields.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Citations explorer tab inside the Lexicon Browser panel.
layer1.why: Citations track the provenance of information used in grounded responses. Use this to verify sources, run health checks, and manage citation records.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the Lexicon Browser panel in citations mode. Auto-loads citations on first visit.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Plugins panel showing all discovered plugins and their connection status.
layer1.why: Use this to see which plugins are active, connect or disconnect plugins, and check their health.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the plugins panel and rescans the plugins directory for current status.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Nodes panel for managing LAN-based distributed computing with other Forest AI machines.
layer1.why: Use this to discover, pair, and manage remote nodes for distributed job execution and file access.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the nodes panel and loads local node info plus any registered remote nodes.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Data Streams panel where you ingest content from Gutenberg, local files, web pages, and RSS feeds into the data lake.
layer1.why: Data streams feed the retrieval system. More quality data means better grounded inference.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the streams panel and initializes all four stream modules with their current manifest stats.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Monitoring panel showing system health, resource usage, and observability data.
layer1.why: Use this to check CPU, memory, and GPU usage, and to monitor Forest AI service health.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the monitoring panel and starts polling for live metrics.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Settings panel where you configure the server URL, display preferences, and auto-refresh behavior.
layer1.why: Use this to change the Forest AI server address, toggle auto-refresh, or save your configuration.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the settings panel showing current configuration values.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Connections panel where you manage external API provider keys and test connectivity.
layer1.why: If you use external providers like OpenAI or Claude, configure their API keys and connection settings here.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the connections panel showing server health checks and provider configuration.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Dev Routing panel where you configure the message processing pipeline.
layer1.why: Routing controls which stages your messages flow through: context injection, retrieval gate, model call, and plugin chain. Use this to customize the pipeline.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the routing panel and loads the active routing profile. Only visible when dev mode is enabled.
layer1.avoid: This is an advanced feature. If you're not customizing the pipeline, the default profile works fine.

layer1.what: Opens the Artifacts panel showing files created by LLM tool calls.
layer1.why: When the AI uses tools that produce files, they appear here. Use this to view, download, or manage generated artifacts.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the artifacts panel and loads the list of DPAPI-encrypted artifacts.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Tool Workshop where you create and manage custom tools the AI can invoke.
layer1.why: Custom tools extend the AI's capabilities. Create PowerShell, Python, Executable, or HTTP tools that the model can call during conversations.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the tools panel and loads your tool inventory.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Opens the Learning Center where you can start guided lesson packs.
layer1.why: Lesson packs walk you through Forest AI concepts step by step, with interactive Q&A and progress tracking.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the learning center and loads available packs plus any saved sessions.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Navigation is always safe.

layer1.what: Tests the connection to the LLM server and loads available models into the dropdown.
layer1.why: You need a working connection before you can send any messages. Click this after starting the server or if the connection drops.
layer1.consequence: On success, the model dropdown populates and the status turns green. On failure, you see a red error status with details.
layer1.avoid: If the LLM server is not running, this will show a connection error. Start the server first.

layer1.what: Sends your prompt to the connected language model and streams the response.
layer1.why: This is how you talk to the AI. Type a prompt, then click Send or press Ctrl+Enter.
layer1.consequence: The model receives your prompt plus conversation history and any active context. The response streams in token by token and is logged to your daily thread file.
layer1.avoid: Don't send if no model is connected (status shows 'Not Connected'). Connect first.

layer1.what: Chooses which language model processes your prompts.
layer1.why: Different models have different strengths. qwen2.5:32b is the default general-purpose model. gpt-oss models are available for specialized tasks.
layer1.consequence: Changing the model affects all subsequent messages. Previous messages were generated by whichever model was selected at the time.
layer1.avoid: If only one model is loaded in Ollama, there is nothing to change here.

layer1.what: The text area where you type your message to the AI.
layer1.why: This is your main input. Type a question, instruction, or conversation. Press Send or Ctrl+Enter to submit.
layer1.consequence: Text here is sent to the model along with your conversation history, system identity, and any active 6-1-6 context.
layer1.avoid: Sending an empty prompt will be rejected.

layer1.what: Upload any file to the governed artifacts store (artifacts/user/ directory).
layer1.why: Share documents, images, data files, or code with the system. Uploaded files are stored alongside AI-generated artifacts and are fully audited.
layer1.consequence: The file is written to artifacts/user/ via the gateway with a full audit trail. It appears in the Artifacts panel for browsing, download, and deletion. Maximum 10MB per file.
layer1.avoid: Do not upload sensitive credentials or keys — use the Connections panel for API keys instead.

layer1.what: Clears the visible chat messages from the display.
layer1.why: Use this for a clean screen when starting a new topic or when the chat gets long.
layer1.consequence: Only clears the display. Your conversation history is preserved in the daily thread file. Refresh the page to restore from saved history.
layer1.avoid: Don't use if you need to reference recent messages visible on screen.

layer1.what: Sets message execution to pipeline mode where your prompt flows through the configured routing stages.
layer1.why: Pipeline mode activates context injection, retrieval gating, and plugin chain processing as defined in your routing profile.
layer1.consequence: Messages are processed through all enabled routing stages before reaching the model. This enables grounding, context injection, and other pipeline features.
layer1.avoid: If you want raw model interaction without any pipeline processing, use Direct mode instead.

layer1.what: Sets message execution to direct mode, bypassing all routing pipeline stages.
layer1.why: Direct mode sends your prompt straight to the model with no context injection, retrieval, or plugin processing.
layer1.consequence: Your message goes directly to the model. No 6-1-6 context, no grounding, no pipeline stages. Useful for simple conversations or debugging.
layer1.avoid: If you need grounded responses or context-aware answers, use Pipeline mode instead.

layer1.what: Selects the conversation mode: LLM (standard), Reasoning (step-by-step), Grounded (citation-backed), Wolf Analysis (deep analysis), or Hybrid.
layer1.why: Different modes activate different processing strategies. Reasoning mode triggers chain-of-thought. Grounded mode forces citation attachment.
layer1.consequence: Changes how the AI processes your next message. The mode is sent as metadata with each prompt.
layer1.avoid: Hybrid mode is not yet fully implemented. Stick to LLM or Grounded for production use.

layer1.what: Enables or disables the AI's ability to invoke registered tools during conversations.
layer1.why: When enabled, the model can call PowerShell, Python, HTTP, or executable tools you've defined in the Tool Workshop.
layer1.consequence: When ON, the tool manifest is included in the system prompt so the model knows what tools are available. When OFF, tools are hidden from the model.
layer1.avoid: Disable tools if you don't want the AI to execute any system commands or scripts.

layer1.what: Enables the continue feature, which lets you ask the AI to extend or fix its last response.
layer1.why: If the model was cut off, gave an incomplete answer, or needs correction, use Continue to prompt it to keep going with optional extra context.
layer1.consequence: When ON, shows the continue context area where you can paste errors or add context before hitting Continue.
layer1.avoid: If continue produces identical responses, a loop warning will appear and auto-stop.

layer1.what: Sends a continuation request to the model, optionally with extra context from the continue input area.
layer1.why: Use this when the AI's response was truncated or you want it to elaborate. Paste an error message or correction in the text area first for guided continuation.
layer1.consequence: Re-sends the conversation with a continuation prompt. The model picks up where it left off or adjusts based on your extra context.
layer1.avoid: Don't spam continue — if the model produces the same output, loop detection will stop it.

layer1.what: Opens the Chat Explorer day picker in the citations pane, showing your conversation history organized by date.
layer1.why: Browse previous days' conversations read-only. Useful for reviewing past discussions or finding specific exchanges.
layer1.consequence: Replaces the citations pane with a list of days. Click a day to view that day's messages. Click Back to return to citations.
layer1.avoid: Chat Explorer is read-only. You cannot edit or delete past conversations from here.

layer1.what: Temperature controls how creative or predictable the AI's responses are.
layer1.why: Lower values (0.0-0.3) give focused, repeatable answers good for factual work. Higher values (0.7+) give variety and exploration good for brainstorming.
layer1.consequence: Moving it left makes responses more deterministic. Moving it right makes them more surprising. At 0.0, the same prompt always gives the same answer.
layer1.avoid: Don't set above 1.2 unless you want unpredictable output. Above 1.5, responses may become incoherent.

relationships: Works with Top-P -- raising both amplifies randomness.
common_mistakes: Setting to 2.0 and wondering why output is garbage.
Setting to 0.0 and expecting creative writing.
layer2.typical_workflow: Most users keep this at 0.5-0.7 for general use. Drop to 0.1 for precise tasks.

layer3.config_path: Runtime only -- sent per-request to Ollama /api/generate
layer3.edge_cases: At exactly 0.0, top_p is ignored (greedy decoding). Some backends treat 0.0 as 'use default' not 'deterministic'.

layer1.what: Top-P controls the diversity of token selection by limiting the cumulative probability pool.
layer1.why: At 0.9, the model considers the smallest set of tokens whose combined probability is 90%. Lower values narrow the pool, higher values widen it.
layer1.consequence: Lower values make output more focused on likely tokens. Higher values allow more variety. At 1.0, all tokens are considered.
layer1.avoid: Avoid setting both temperature and top-p to extreme values simultaneously. They amplify each other.

layer1.what: Sets the maximum number of tokens the model can generate in a single response.
layer1.why: Use this to limit response length. Short for quick answers, long for detailed explanations or code generation.
layer1.consequence: The model stops generating when it hits this limit, even mid-sentence. Higher values allow longer responses but take more time.
layer1.avoid: Setting this too low (under 100) may cut off useful responses. Setting it very high (8000+) with a slow model means long wait times.

layer1.what: Selects which model the inference parameter sliders apply to.
layer1.why: Different models may benefit from different parameter values. This lets you maintain separate tuning profiles per model.
layer1.consequence: Changing this loads the stored parameter profile for that model, or defaults if none is saved yet.
layer1.avoid: If you only have one model loaded, this just reflects the active model.

layer1.what: Sends your current slider settings to the LLM server so they take effect on the next message.
layer1.why: Slider changes are local until you apply them. This pushes the configuration to the running server.
layer1.consequence: All subsequent model responses use these inference parameters. The active config badge updates to reflect the applied values.
layer1.avoid: Don't apply if you're still experimenting with sliders. Changes take effect on the next message you send.

layer1.what: Penalizes the model for repeating tokens it has already used, reducing repetitive output.
layer1.why: Higher values discourage the model from reusing words and phrases. Useful when responses become loops or echo themselves.
layer1.consequence: At 1.0 (no penalty), repetition is uninhibited. At 1.1-1.2, mild discouragement. Above 1.3, the model actively avoids repeating itself.
layer1.avoid: Don't set above 1.5 — the model may produce incoherent output as it strains to avoid any repetition.

layer1.what: Sets the random seed for reproducible generation. Use -1 for a different seed each time.
layer1.why: A fixed seed with temperature 0 gives identical output for the same prompt. Useful for testing and comparing changes.
layer1.consequence: With a fixed seed, re-sending the same prompt produces the same response. With -1, each response varies naturally.
layer1.avoid: Seed only guarantees determinism with temperature 0. At higher temperatures, outputs still vary slightly.

layer1.what: Quick-apply preset parameter profiles. Each preset configures temperature, top-p, repeat penalty, and max tokens in one click.
layer1.why: Presets save you from adjusting individual sliders for common use cases: Precise (factual), Workhorse (balanced output), Explorer (creative), Locked (deterministic).
layer1.consequence: Clicking a preset immediately updates all sliders to that profile's values. The Custom preset preserves your manual adjustments.
layer1.avoid: Presets overwrite your current slider positions. If you've tuned custom values, click Custom first to preserve them.

layer1.what: Rules injected into the system prompt that constrain model output: END marker, essentials checklist, bullet mode, and custom rules.
layer1.why: The generation contract ensures the model 'lands the plane' — finishes cleanly, hits required points, and follows your formatting rules.
layer1.consequence: Checked options are appended to the system prompt before each message. The model sees them as instructions it must follow.
layer1.avoid: Too many strict rules can make responses feel formulaic. Use sparingly for production conversations.

layer1.what: Resets all inference parameters to their default values (Balanced preset).
layer1.why: If you've made changes you want to undo, this is the fast path back to safe defaults.
layer1.consequence: All sliders, seed, and generation contract options revert to defaults. This does not auto-apply to the server — click Apply to Server after resetting.
layer1.avoid: Your custom settings are lost immediately. There is no undo.

layer1.what: Ingests public domain books from Project Gutenberg into the LakeSpeak data lake.
layer1.why: Feed high-quality text into the retrieval system so the AI can ground responses in real source material.
layer1.consequence: Downloads book text, chunks it, extracts anchors and relations, and stores everything in the LakeSpeak index. The book becomes searchable for grounded inference.
layer1.avoid: Don't ingest massive volumes at once. Each book goes through the full pipeline. Start with a few to verify quality.

layer1.what: Ingests text files from your local filesystem into the LakeSpeak data lake.
layer1.why: Use this to index your own documents, notes, or text files so the AI can reference them during grounded conversations.
layer1.consequence: Reads files from the specified paths, chunks the content, and stores it in the LakeSpeak index. Supports recursive directory scanning.
layer1.avoid: Don't point this at huge directories without checking file counts first. Use the manifest to see what will be ingested.

layer1.what: Ingests web pages into the LakeSpeak data lake by fetching and parsing HTML content.
layer1.why: Use this to index web content so the AI can ground responses in specific online sources.
layer1.consequence: Fetches the URL, strips boilerplate HTML, chunks the text content, and stores it in the LakeSpeak index.
layer1.avoid: Respect rate limits. Don't scrape sites that prohibit it. Use the preview to verify content quality before ingesting.

layer1.what: Ingests articles from RSS/Atom feeds into the LakeSpeak data lake.
layer1.why: Use this to continuously index content from news feeds, blogs, or other syndicated sources.
layer1.consequence: Fetches feed entries, extracts article content, chunks it, and stores it in the LakeSpeak index. The Latest field controls how many recent entries to process.
layer1.avoid: Check the feed URL is valid first. Use Preview to verify content extraction works before ingesting.

layer1.what: Simulates the ingestion process without writing anything to the data lake.
layer1.why: Preview what will happen: how many chunks, words, and anchors a source will produce, without committing any data.
layer1.consequence: Returns statistics (chunk count, word count, anchor count) but does not modify any indexes or storage. Completely safe.
layer1.avoid: No reason to avoid it. Dry run is always safe.

layer1.what: Runs the full ingestion pipeline: fetches content, chunks it, extracts anchors, and writes everything to the data lake.
layer1.why: This is the real operation — after previewing or dry-running, click Ingest to commit the data to LakeSpeak.
layer1.consequence: Content is permanently added to the data lake. The manifest updates with the new entry. The data becomes available for grounded inference.
layer1.avoid: Use Dry Run first to verify what will happen. Ingest writes to disk and is harder to undo.

layer1.what: Fetches and displays a sample of the source content without ingesting it.
layer1.why: Check that the content looks correct before committing to a full ingest. Useful for verifying web scrapes and Gutenberg book selections.
layer1.consequence: Shows a text preview in the panel. Nothing is written to disk or the data lake.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Preview is read-only.

layer1.what: Saves the current pipeline stage configuration to the active routing profile.
layer1.why: Routing controls which stages messages flow through: context injection, retrieval gate, model call, and plugin chain. Save preserves your pipeline setup.
layer1.consequence: Overwrites the current profile's settings in forest.config.json. The pipeline order and per-stage config are persisted.
layer1.avoid: If you're experimenting, use Save As to create a named profile instead of overwriting the default.

layer1.what: Saves the current pipeline configuration as a new named profile.
layer1.why: Named profiles let you maintain multiple pipeline configurations and switch between them. Useful for different workflows.
layer1.consequence: Creates a new named profile in the routing profiles directory. Does not overwrite the default or any existing profile.
layer1.avoid: Profile names must match the pattern: letters, numbers, hyphens, underscores, max 64 characters.

layer1.what: Lists all saved routing profiles. Select one to load its pipeline configuration.
layer1.why: Switch between different pipeline setups for different workflows without reconfiguring stages manually each time.
layer1.consequence: Loading a profile replaces the current pipeline stage configuration with the saved one. Unsaved changes to the current profile are lost.
layer1.avoid: Save your current profile before loading a different one if you want to keep your changes.

layer1.what: Resets the pipeline configuration to the factory default profile.
layer1.why: If you've made changes that broke routing or want a clean slate, this restores the standard pipeline.
layer1.consequence: Overwrites the current pipeline with default stages. Your custom changes are lost. Named profiles are not affected.
layer1.avoid: Save your current profile first with Save As if you might want it back.

layer1.what: Switches between Pipeline mode (messages flow through routing stages) and Direct mode (messages go straight to the model).
layer1.why: Pipeline mode enables grounding, context injection, and plugin processing. Direct mode is raw model interaction.
layer1.consequence: This toggle syncs with the same control in the chat panel. Changing it here changes it everywhere.
layer1.avoid: Direct mode skips all pipeline features. Use Pipeline for production conversations.

layer1.what: Saves the system identity (who the AI is) and archive instruction (how it handles retrieved history).
layer1.why: The system identity shapes every response. The archive instruction defines how the AI uses retrieved conversation history from previous sessions.
layer1.consequence: Writes to forest.config.json. All subsequent messages use the updated identity and archive instruction.
layer1.avoid: Be thoughtful about identity changes. They affect the tone and behavior of every response the AI gives.

layer1.what: Saves your user profile including display name, role, working context, communication style, expertise level, and notes.
layer1.why: The user profile gives the AI context about who you are and how you prefer to communicate. This helps it tailor responses to your needs.
layer1.consequence: Writes profile data to forest.config.json. The AI's system prompt includes your profile in subsequent messages.
layer1.avoid: No risk in saving. The profile only affects how the AI addresses you and calibrates its responses.

layer1.what: Removes all user profile data, resetting it to empty defaults.
layer1.why: Use this if you want to start fresh or remove personal context from the system.
layer1.consequence: Clears display name, role, context, notes, style, and level. The AI will respond without user-specific context until you fill it in again.
layer1.avoid: This is immediate. There is no undo. Save a backup of your profile text if you might want it back.

layer1.what: Saves an encrypted API key for the selected external provider (OpenAI, Claude, etc.).
layer1.why: External providers require API keys for authentication. This stores the key securely using DPAPI encryption on your local machine.
layer1.consequence: The key is encrypted at rest and only decrypted when making API calls to that provider. You can use the provider's models after saving a valid key.
layer1.avoid: Never share your API keys. They are stored locally and encrypted, but treat them as sensitive credentials.

layer1.what: Tests connectivity to the selected external provider by making a lightweight API call.
layer1.why: Verify your API key works and the provider is reachable before using it for chat. Tests the /v1/models endpoint.
layer1.consequence: Makes a single API call to verify connectivity. Shows success or failure with a descriptive message. Does not consume meaningful API credits.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Testing is free or negligible cost.

layer1.what: Disconnects the external provider and optionally deletes the stored API key.
layer1.why: Use this if you no longer want to use a provider, or if you need to rotate your API key.
layer1.consequence: The provider becomes unavailable for chat. If you delete the key, you will need to re-enter it to reconnect.
layer1.avoid: Make sure you are not in the middle of an active conversation using this provider's model.

layer1.what: Toggles LAN discovery to find other Forest AI nodes on your local network.
layer1.why: Discovery broadcasts on your LAN to find other machines running the Forest Node daemon. Found nodes appear in the Discovered Nodes list.
layer1.consequence: When active, the system listens for and announces its presence on the local network. When stopped, no network traffic is generated.
layer1.avoid: Don't run discovery on untrusted networks. Only use on your own LAN.

layer1.what: Opens a modal to register a remote Forest AI node by IP address and port.
layer1.why: If a node is not discovered automatically, you can connect to it manually by entering its IP address and daemon port.
layer1.consequence: Adds the node to your registered nodes list. The node must be running the Forest Node daemon and be reachable on the specified port (default 5052).
layer1.avoid: Don't connect to nodes you don't trust. The connection requires HMAC pairing before any operations are allowed.

layer1.what: Reloads the node list, checking status and connectivity of all registered nodes.
layer1.why: Nodes may come online or go offline. Refresh updates the status indicators for all registered nodes.
layer1.consequence: Queries each registered node for its current status. Updates the online/offline indicators and system info.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only status check.

layer1.what: Sends a heartbeat ping to all registered nodes to check if they are alive and responding.
layer1.why: A quick health check that verifies network connectivity and daemon responsiveness without fetching full status.
layer1.consequence: Each node receives a lightweight ping. Online nodes respond immediately. Offline nodes time out after a few seconds.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Heartbeat is a passive health probe.

layer1.what: Starts the 3-step mobile device pairing wizard: USB detection, Windows Hello approval, and enrollment bundle delivery.
layer1.why: Pair your phone to access Forest AI from a mobile browser with Ed25519-signed authentication.
layer1.consequence: Creates an encrypted enrollment bundle containing the device's credentials. The phone can then authenticate to the UI server over TLS.
layer1.avoid: Requires a USB connection and Windows Hello biometric approval. Make sure your phone is connected via USB cable.

layer1.what: Sets the file browser to locked mode — the most restrictive access level. Only pre-approved paths are visible.
layer1.why: Locked mode is the default and safest access level for browsing remote node files.
layer1.consequence: Only files in the node's allowlist are visible. You cannot navigate outside approved directories.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is the default safe mode.

layer1.what: Sets the file browser to allowlist mode — expands visible paths to the full allowlist configured on the remote node.
layer1.why: Allowlist mode gives broader read access while still restricting navigation to approved directories.
layer1.consequence: All paths in the node's allowlist configuration become browsable. You can read files but not write unless write mode is separately enabled.
layer1.avoid: Allowlist paths are set by the remote node's config. You cannot add paths from the browser.

layer1.what: Requests full filesystem access on the remote node. Requires a 30-second arming countdown as deliberate friction.
layer1.why: Full access lets you browse the entire remote filesystem. The countdown is intentional UX friction, not a security gate — it ensures you meant to do this.
layer1.consequence: After the 30-second countdown, the full filesystem becomes browsable (read-only by default). Access expires after a TTL. You can lock immediately at any time.
layer1.avoid: This is the highest access level. Only use when you genuinely need to browse outside the allowlist.

layer1.what: Immediately revokes elevated file browser access and returns to locked mode.
layer1.why: Use this when you're done browsing in allowlist or full mode. Returns to the safest access level instantly.
layer1.consequence: Access drops to locked mode. Any open file previews close. The TTL timer is cancelled.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Locking is always safe.

layer1.what: Toggles write access in the file browser, enabling file upload and folder creation on the remote node.
layer1.why: By default, the file browser is read-only. Enable write mode to upload files or create directories on the remote node.
layer1.consequence: When ON, Upload and New Folder buttons become visible. Files and folders can be created in the current directory.
layer1.avoid: Write operations modify the remote filesystem. Make sure you're in the correct directory before uploading.

layer1.what: Uploads a file from your local machine to the current directory on the remote node.
layer1.why: Transfer files to a remote node for processing, configuration, or data sharing.
layer1.consequence: The file is uploaded to the remote node's current browsed directory. Overwrites files with the same name.
layer1.avoid: Requires write mode to be enabled. Check the current directory path in the breadcrumb before uploading.

layer1.what: Creates a new folder in the current directory on the remote node.
layer1.why: Organize files on the remote node by creating directories as needed.
layer1.consequence: Prompts for a folder name and creates it in the currently browsed directory.
layer1.avoid: Requires write mode to be enabled. Folder names must be valid for the remote OS.

layer1.what: Saves your current settings to the configuration file.
layer1.why: Settings changes only persist when you explicitly save them. Otherwise they reset on next launch.
layer1.consequence: Writes to forest.config.json. The server URL, display preferences, and auto-refresh setting are persisted.
layer1.avoid: Don't save if you are testing a temporary server URL you don't want to keep.

layer1.what: Reloads the DocuMap statistics: queued documents, mapped documents, grove anchors, and system-verified counts.
layer1.why: Stats may be stale after ingesting new data or mapping documents. Refresh pulls the latest numbers from the bridge.
layer1.consequence: Fetches fresh stats from /api/documap/stats and updates all the stat cards. No data is modified.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only operation.

layer1.what: Filters the Data Grove document list by filename or title as you type.
layer1.why: When you have many mapped documents, searching lets you quickly find specific ones without scrolling.
layer1.consequence: Filters the visible list in real time. No API calls — filtering happens in the browser on already-loaded data.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Search is local filtering.

layer1.what: Reloads the list of mapped documents from the Data Grove.
layer1.why: New documents may have been mapped since the last load. Refresh fetches the latest list.
layer1.consequence: Fetches documents from /api/documap/docs and rebuilds the document list display.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only refresh.

layer1.what: Opens the interactive Cytoscape graph viewer showing anchor relationships for the selected document.
layer1.why: Visualize how anchors connect within a document. Useful for understanding the 6-1-6 mapping topology.
layer1.consequence: Opens a full-screen overlay with an interactive force-directed graph. You can pan, zoom, and export.
layer1.avoid: Very large documents may produce slow-to-render graphs. Start with smaller documents.

layer1.what: Uploads the file or pasted text and runs it through the 6-1-6 mapping pipeline.
layer1.why: This is the primary action for adding new content to the Data Grove. Your text is tokenized, anchors are extracted, and the map is stored.
layer1.consequence: The document is processed synchronously. A progress bar shows chunk-by-chunk status. On completion, coverage stats and unmapped word count are displayed.
layer1.avoid: Very large documents take longer. Check the filename field is set before uploading.

layer1.what: Downloads the mapping result of the last processed document as a JSON file.
layer1.why: Export the raw 6-1-6 mapping data for analysis, backup, or external tool use.
layer1.consequence: Triggers a browser download of the JSON file containing all anchor windows, token IDs, and mapping metadata.
layer1.avoid: Only available after a successful mapping. If no mapping has been done, there is nothing to download.

layer1.what: Click or drag-and-drop a file to upload it for 6-1-6 mapping.
layer1.why: Upload .txt, .md, or .json files containing text you want to map to the canonical lexicon.
layer1.consequence: The file contents are loaded into the upload form. You still need to click Upload & Map to process it.
layer1.avoid: Only text-based files are supported. Binary files, images, and PDFs are not handled.

layer1.what: Search the lexicon by word, binary address, hex code, or tone signature.
layer1.why: Find specific entries in your word database. The search queries the backend and returns matching entries as explorer cards.
layer1.consequence: Results appear in the card grid below. Each card shows the word, its binary address, hex code, tone, and frequency data.
layer1.avoid: Very short queries (1-2 characters) may return too many results. Be specific.

layer1.what: Shows entries from all lexicon packs (canonical + medical combined).
layer1.why: The default view. Removes any active pack filter and shows the full lexicon.
layer1.consequence: Reloads results showing all packs. The filter indicator in the context strip clears.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is the default state.

layer1.what: Filters the lexicon to show only entries from the canonical word pack.
layer1.why: The canonical pack contains core English vocabulary mapped through the 6-1-6 pipeline.
layer1.consequence: Results, letter distribution, and browse operations are scoped to canonical entries only.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Click ALL to remove the filter.

layer1.what: Filters the lexicon to show only entries from the medical terminology pack.
layer1.why: The medical pack contains specialized healthcare and clinical vocabulary.
layer1.consequence: Results, letter distribution, and browse operations are scoped to medical entries only.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Click ALL to remove the filter.

layer1.what: Loads the 30 most frequently occurring words in the lexicon, sorted by frequency count.
layer1.why: See which words appear most often across your mapped documents. Useful for understanding your corpus composition.
layer1.consequence: Replaces the current card grid with top-frequency entries. Each card shows the word's frequency count.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only browse operation.

layer1.what: Loads 24 randomly selected entries from the lexicon.
layer1.why: Discover words you might not find by searching. A quick way to explore the breadth of your lexicon.
layer1.consequence: Replaces the card grid with randomly selected entries. Click again for a different set.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only browse operation.

layer1.what: Shows the 30 most recently mapped words, ordered by mapping timestamp.
layer1.why: Track what was just added to the lexicon. Useful after running a mapping job to see the new entries.
layer1.consequence: Replaces the card grid with recently mapped entries. The most recent appears first.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only browse operation.

layer1.what: Lists all lexicon partition files on disk, showing file sizes and entry counts.
layer1.why: The lexicon is stored across multiple partition files. This view lets you inspect the physical storage layout.
layer1.consequence: Shows a list of partition files. Click one to browse its contents. Each file holds a subset of the lexicon.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only view of the storage structure.

layer1.what: Shows words that appeared in mapped documents but could not be matched to any canonical lexicon entry.
layer1.why: Unmatched words represent gaps in your lexicon. Review them to decide if they should be added or are noise.
layer1.consequence: Displays a list of unmatched words with their occurrence counts. High-frequency unmatched words may need to be added to the lexicon.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a diagnostic read-only view.

layer1.what: DANGER: Deletes all entries from the canonical lexicon pack. This is a brain operation.
layer1.why: Only use this if you need to completely rebuild the canonical lexicon from scratch.
layer1.consequence: All canonical entries are permanently deleted. The lexicon must be repopulated by re-mapping documents. Requires Windows Hello + confirmation phrase.
layer1.avoid: This is irreversible without a backup restore. Make absolutely sure you have a backup before proceeding.

layer1.what: DANGER: Returns canonical words back to the unmapped pool for re-processing.
layer1.why: Use this if words were incorrectly mapped and need to be re-evaluated through the pipeline.
layer1.consequence: Selected canonical entries lose their canonical status and return to the unmapped word pool. Requires Windows Hello + confirmation phrase.
layer1.avoid: This is a destructive brain operation. Only use when you know specific words were mis-mapped.

layer1.what: DANGER: Bulk-imports a word list into the canonical lexicon from an external file.
layer1.why: Seed the lexicon with a large word list instead of mapping documents one at a time.
layer1.consequence: All words in the imported file are added to the canonical pack. Duplicates are skipped. Requires Windows Hello + confirmation phrase.
layer1.avoid: Large imports can significantly change the lexicon composition. Review the word list before importing.

layer1.what: Search citations by coordinate, subject tag, note text, or cite_id.
layer1.why: Find specific citations in your citation store without scrolling through the full list.
layer1.consequence: Filters the visible citation cards in real time as you type. Results match against coordinate, subject, note, and cite_id fields.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Search is a client-side filter on loaded data.

layer1.what: Shows citations from all sources (UI, System, and LakeSpeak combined).
layer1.why: The default view. Removes any active source filter and shows all citation records.
layer1.consequence: All citation cards become visible. The filter indicator in the context strip clears.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is the default state.

layer1.what: Filters citations to show only those created from the UI (user-attached citations).
layer1.why: UI citations are manually attached by you during conversations using the cite overlay.
layer1.consequence: Only UI-sourced citation cards are shown. Cards have green left borders.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Click ALL to remove the filter.

layer1.what: Filters citations to show only those created by the system (auto-generated citations).
layer1.why: System citations are automatically created by the pipeline during grounded inference when the model references archived content.
layer1.consequence: Only system-sourced citation cards are shown. Cards have cyan left borders.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Click ALL to remove the filter.

layer1.what: Filters citations to show only those linked to LakeSpeak data lake content.
layer1.why: LakeSpeak citations reference content from ingested data streams (Gutenberg, local files, web scrapes, RSS feeds).
layer1.consequence: Only LakeSpeak-sourced citation cards are shown. Cards have gold left borders.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Click ALL to remove the filter.

layer1.what: Loads or reloads the full citation list from the citation store.
layer1.why: Fetches all citations (paginated) and renders them as explorer cards. Use this to refresh after changes.
layer1.consequence: Replaces the card grid with all citations. The first page loads immediately; use Load More for additional pages.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only fetch.

layer1.what: Loads the next page of citations, appending to the current display.
layer1.why: Citations are paginated to keep the UI responsive. Click Load More to fetch additional entries.
layer1.consequence: Fetches the next batch and appends citation cards to the grid. If no more citations exist, a message indicates the end.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Pagination is automatic.

layer1.what: Runs a 30-day citation health audit, checking for orphaned, stale, or broken citation records.
layer1.why: Over time, citations may become orphaned if source messages are deleted, or stale if the underlying archive changes.
layer1.consequence: Generates a health report showing total citations, broken references, orphaned records, and overall health status.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Health check is read-only. It reports problems but does not fix them.

layer1.what: Reloads the list of artifacts (files created by LLM tool calls).
layer1.why: New artifacts may have been created during conversations. Refresh updates the list.
layer1.consequence: Fetches the current artifact inventory and rebuilds the sidebar list. Artifacts are DPAPI-encrypted on disk.
layer1.avoid: No risk. This is a read-only refresh.

layer1.what: Rescans the plugins directory and updates the plugin list with current status.
layer1.why: If you've added, removed, or modified plugins, rescan discovers the changes.
layer1.consequence: Re-reads the plugins directory, checks each plugin's health, and refreshes the list and pipeline lane.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Rescan does not modify plugins.

layer1.what: Opens the tool creation form to define a new custom tool the AI can invoke.
layer1.why: Extend the AI's capabilities by creating PowerShell, Python, Executable, or HTTP tools.
layer1.consequence: Opens the editor in the detail pane. Fill in the tool name, type, description, and implementation, then save.
layer1.avoid: Tool implementations run with your system privileges. Only create tools you trust.

layer1.what: Reloads the tool inventory from the tools directory.
layer1.why: If you've manually added or modified tool definition files, refresh picks up the changes.
layer1.consequence: Re-reads the tools directory and updates the sidebar list with current tool definitions.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Refresh is read-only.

layer1.what: Installs a new lesson pack from a local folder path.
layer1.why: Add custom or downloaded lesson packs to your Learning Center library.
layer1.consequence: Copies the pack from the specified path into the chat_packs directory. The pack appears in Available Packs after installation.
layer1.avoid: Make sure the path points to a valid pack folder with a manifest.json.

layer1.what: Advances to the next section or question in the current lesson pack.
layer1.why: Move through the lesson content step by step. Progress is saved automatically.
layer1.consequence: Loads the next section content and updates the progress bar. If you're at the last section, the lesson completes.
layer1.avoid: Make sure you've reviewed the current section before advancing. You can always go back via the outline sidebar.

layer1.what: Opens the Local Chat panel with the current lesson context pre-loaded, so you can ask the AI questions about the material.
layer1.why: If something in the lesson is unclear, switch to chat to have an interactive conversation about it with full lesson context.
layer1.consequence: Switches to the chat panel with a breadcrumb linking back to the lesson. The pack context is included in the system prompt.
layer1.avoid: No risk. Your lesson progress is preserved.

layer1.what: Resets the current lesson pack to the beginning, clearing all progress.
layer1.why: Start over from the first section if you want to review the material from scratch.
layer1.consequence: All section progress is cleared. The lesson starts from section 1. This cannot be undone.
layer1.avoid: Your progress is permanently lost. Only reset if you're sure you want to start over.
